narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam based on academic and personal factors from past students. The model estimated a 52.5% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as schools prioritize admitting those predicted to pass with high confidence. This decision hinged primarily on three key factors from your profile: your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and Year 3 school decile ranking. Your LSAT score of 32, notably below the typical 36.7 average, was the strongest driver pushing the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model a potential challenge in handling the rigorous reasoning demands of the bar. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7, lower than the average 3.21 but still a positive factor here, worked in your favor by suggesting some foundational academic resilience that nudged the odds toward passing. Similarly, your Year 3 decile of 4—below the average 5.53—provided another modest boost toward success, indicating you held your own relative to peers later in your studies despite early hurdles like your Year 1 decile of 4. Being a full-time student, which aligns with the vast majority of applicants, didn't sway the outcome much either way. Overall, while your GPA and later decile offered some counterbalance, the LSAT's influence outweighed them, placing your profile among those the model sees as higher-risk compared to typical admitted students who boast stronger standardized test performance. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer or your upper-middle-class stability, but a data-driven flag on bar passage odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses or highlighting upward trends in your academics, like that Year 3 decile, would also help. Reach out to admissions advisors for targeted feedback, and explore programs with holistic reviews that might weigh your full-time commitment and background more favorably next cycle.
